facts: Originated in Ankara region of Ottoman Turkey.
Treasured by Ottoman royalty as silky white longhairs.
Given as prestigious diplomatic gifts in elite courts.
First exported to Europe; prized by Victorian fanciers.
Used as foundation stock for early Persian breeding programs.
Fell out of fashion as Persian-type cats took the spotlight.
Heavy interbreeding with Persians nearly erased the pure line.
Purebred Angoras became almost impossible to find outside Turkey.
By 1917 Turkish government began collecting whites for protection.
Ankara Zoo ran a strict breeding program to preserve pure whites.
American servicemen rediscovered the breed at the zoo.
Export of white Angoras was forbidden by Turkish law.
Walter and Liesa Grant imported Yildiz and Yildizcik in Nov 1962.
CFA granted provisional status in 1968, championship in 1970.
Full CFA recognition for white Angoras came in 1972.
Colored Turkish Angoras accepted by CFA in 1978.
TICA recognized the breed in 1979.
Breed standards expanded; show presence grew worldwide.
Declared a National Treasure of Turkey.
Ankara Zoo and Ataturk Forest Farm still maintain pure lines.
Recognized by CFA and TICA; remains rare in the UK.

today.summary: The Turkish Angora is a thriving but still uncommon breed, protected as a Turkish National Treasure and recognized by major Western registries.
facts: Silky single-coat longhair with no dense undercoat, low matting.
Athletic, intelligent, playful into adulthood; called the cat-world race car.
Recognized by CFA, TICA; not yet by UK's GCCF.
Pure lines preserved at Ankara Zoo and Ataturk Forest Farm.
Comes in many colors today, but classic odd-eyed white is iconic.
